summary: German polymath and rationalist philosopher of the 17th-18th centuries. Co-discovered calculus independently of Newton. Author of Monadology, Discourse on Metaphysics, Theodicy, and New Essays on Human Understanding. Developed the concepts of monads ('windowless' simple substances), the identity of indiscernibles, pre-established harmony, the principle of sufficient reason, and the claim that we live in the 'best of all possible worlds' (satirized as Pangloss in Voltaire's Candide).

works.name: Monads and the Monadology
works.description: The Monadology (1714) describes the universe as composed of 'windowless' simple substances called monads, which have perception and appetite but no spatial extension. They exist in a three-tiered hierarchy of entelechies, souls, and spirits, and interact only through God's pre-established harmony. Leibniz borrowed the term 'entelechies' from Aristotle to describe entities with an inner self-directed nature. The 'mill argument' imagines enlarging a thinking machine enough to walk inside it, and David Chalmers claims it inspired the philosophical zombie thought experiment. Anne Conway's 'spirit particles' from her Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy are believed to have inspired the concept of monads. Deleuze interpreted monads as 'folds' in time and space in his book The Fold.

clues.clue: 'windowless' simple substances (monads) — indivisible, without extension, composing all reality
examples: argued that the only beings were 'windowless' simple substances
posited the existence of simple substances that are nonextendable, indivisible, and windowless

clues.clue: pre-established harmony — no real interaction between substances; God has synchronized them
examples: rejected the theory of mind-body interaction in favor of pre-established harmony
replaced causation with his idea of 'pre-established harmony'

clues.clue: mill argument — imagine enlarging a thinking machine to walk inside it like a mill; inspired Chalmers's philosophical zombie
examples: imagines being able to enlarge a thinking machine enough to walk around inside it like a mill
David Chalmers claims that the inspiration for his philosophical zombie thought experiment rests in this thinker's mill argument

examples: posited a tripartite hierarchy of entelechies, souls, and spirits

clues.clue: Anne Conway's 'spirit particles' inspired monads — female Cambridge Platonist influenced by kabbalah
examples: Conway posited 'middle nature' to explain how God communicates with matter made of 'spirit particles,' which many scholars believe to have inspired this German philosopher's idea of monads

clues.clue: petites perceptions — subconscious formulations of perception; e.g., hearing the roar of waves without distinguishing individual drops
examples: claimed that we formulate perceptions subconsciously, which he called petites perceptions

works.name: Identity of Indiscernibles (Leibniz's Law)
works.description: Leibniz's Law states that if two objects share all of the same properties, they must be identical. It was first explicitly formulated in the Discourse on Metaphysics and defended via the principle of sufficient reason in correspondence with Samuel Clarke. Max Black's counterexample of two identical iron spheres in a perfectly symmetric universe is the most famous objection. The 'masked man' fallacy is an incorrect application of the principle. Quine developed a 'maxim of the identification of indiscernibles' based on it. Kant argued against it using raindrops in the 'Amphiboly' section of the Critique of Pure Reason.

examples: this law which states that if two objects have all the same properties, then they must be the same

clues.clue: used to argue against absolute space — if God shifted the universe, nothing would differ
examples: invoked in a passage that argues the universe would be no different if God moved it 'forward in a right line'

works.name: Best of All Possible Worlds and the Theodicy
works.description: Leibniz's Theodicy (Theodicee, 1710) defends philosophical optimism using the principle of sufficient reason, arguing God created the 'best of all possible worlds' — one that is 'simplest in hypotheses and richest in phenomena.' The work ends with a goddess guiding Theodorus to the top hall of an infinite pyramid. Satirized by Voltaire as Dr. Pangloss in Candide. Alvin Plantinga called his assumption that this is the best world 'Leibniz's lapse.' The Discourse on Metaphysics clarifies the claim.

clues.clue: 'best of all possible worlds' — God created the ideal universe; philosophical optimism defended with sufficient reason
examples: claimed that we lived in the best of all possible worlds
tenet of 'philosophical optimism' is defended with the principle of sufficient reason in Theodicee

clues.clue: satirized by Voltaire as Dr. Pangloss in Candide

examples: a goddess guides Theodorus to the top hall of an infinite pyramid

works.name: Discourse on Metaphysics and Truth Theory
works.description: The Discourse on Metaphysics (1686) introduces the 'complete individual concept' — every substance has a concept containing everything that will ever happen to it. Leibniz illustrated this with Alexander the Great's soul and Julius Caesar crossing the Rubicon. The work also distinguishes between necessary truths (grounded in non-contradiction) and contingent truths (grounded in the principle of sufficient reason). Haecceity (from Duns Scotus) is invoked to argue something is only a substance if it has a complete concept.

clues.clue: 'complete individual concept' — every substance's concept specifies everything that will ever happen to it; illustrated by Judas betraying Christ, Alexander the Great, Caesar crossing the Rubicon
examples: used the example of Judas betraying Christ to illustrate his idea that every substance has a 'complete concept'
used Julius Caesar crossing the Rubicon to illustrate a theory of truth where predicates are contained within concepts

clues.clue: necessary truths grounded in non-contradiction; contingent truths grounded in sufficient reason
examples: only necessary truths are grounded on Aristotle's principle of non-contradiction, while contingent truths are grounded in the Principle of Sufficient Reason

clues.clue: principle of sufficient reason — everything happens for a reason; first formulated here
examples: was the first to formulate the principle of sufficient reason
the Principle of Sufficient Reason

works.name: New Essays on Human Understanding
works.description: Leibniz's New Essays on Human Understanding is a chapter-by-chapter refutation of John Locke's Essay Concerning Human Understanding, written as a dialogue between Theophilus (Leibniz) and Philalethes (Locke). It defends innate ideas against Locke's tabula rasa, arguing the mind is not a 'blank tablet' but a 'veined block of marble.' It also contains his exploration of 'petites perceptions' (unconscious perceptions) and the claim 'nothing is in the intellect which was not first in the senses, except the intellect itself.'

clues.clue: chapter-by-chapter refutation of Locke's Essay; Theophilus = Leibniz, Philalethes = Locke
examples: presented himself as Theophilus in a refutation of a contemporary titled the New Essays

clues.clue: mind is not a 'blank tablet' but a 'veined block of marble' — defense of innate ideas

clues.clue: 'nothing is in the intellect which was not first in the senses, except the intellect itself'

works.name: General / Biographical and Calculus
works.description: Common identifiers: German philosopher/polymath, co-discovered calculus independently of Newton, author of Monadology. The Newton-Leibniz calculus controversy involved accusations that Leibniz plagiarized Newton's work on quadrature. Leibniz published the relevant material in Nova Methodus pro maximis et minimis and created the integral symbol. He corresponded with Samuel Clarke (a Newton ally) until his death in 1716, debating absolute vs. relational space. His characteristica universalis was a universal language project drawing on Chinese characters. Bertrand Russell argued (now rejected) that Leibniz kept his 'true philosophy' to himself.

clues.clue: co-discovered calculus independently of Newton; created the integral symbol
examples: pioneered calculus independently of Isaac Newton

examples: drew on Chinese characters in imagining a universal language called the characteristica universalis

examples: Bertrand Russell introduced the now-widely-rejected argument that this philosopher had to toady to his patrons

examples: this philosopher's interest in China, with whom this philosopher wanted to establish a 'commerce of light'

comprehensive_summary:
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716) is among the most heavily clued philosophers in quizbowl, with a rich and varied set of questions spanning metaphysics, logic, mathematics, and political philosophy. The three universal giveaways are: monads ('windowless' simple substances), the best of all possible worlds, and co-discovering calculus with Newton.

The Monadology describes all reality as composed of simple, indivisible, extensionless substances called monads that have perception and appetite but are 'windowless' — they do not interact causally. Instead, God has synchronized them through pre-established harmony. Leibniz borrowed the term 'entelechies' from Aristotle for entities with self-directed inner nature, and organized monads in a hierarchy of entelechies, souls, and spirits. Anne Conway's 'spirit particles' are believed to have inspired the concept. The 'mill argument' (imagine enlarging a thinking machine to walk inside it) inspired David Chalmers's philosophical zombie.

The identity of indiscernibles (Leibniz's Law) states that two objects sharing all properties must be identical. Max Black's famous counterexample posits two iron spheres in a perfectly symmetric universe. Leibniz defended it via the principle of sufficient reason in his correspondence with Samuel Clarke, where he also argued against Newton's absolute space using 'static shift' and 'kinematic shift' arguments.

The Theodicy defends philosophical optimism: God created the best of all possible worlds, one that is 'simplest in hypotheses and richest in phenomena.' Voltaire satirized this as Dr. Pangloss in Candide. Plantinga called the assumption 'Leibniz's lapse.'

The Discourse on Metaphysics introduces the 'complete individual concept' (every substance's concept contains everything that will ever happen to it), illustrated by Judas, Alexander the Great, and Caesar. It distinguishes necessary truths (non-contradiction) from contingent truths (sufficient reason).

New Essays on Human Understanding refutes Locke chapter by chapter, with Leibniz as Theophilus and Locke as Philalethes. It defends innate ideas, comparing the mind to a 'veined block of marble' rather than a blank slate, and introduces 'petites perceptions' (unconscious perceptions).

The calculus priority dispute with Newton is a frequent bonus topic. Leibniz published in Nova Methodus and created the integral symbol. His correspondence with Clarke debated relational vs. absolute space. His characteristica universalis project envisioned a universal language, captured in his exclamation 'Calculemus!' David Lewis's modal realism builds on Leibniz's possible worlds framework.

Concept: the principle that two objects sharing all the same properties must be identical; Max Black's counterexample uses two iron spheres in a symmetric universe
Philosopher: argued we live in the 'best of all possible worlds,' defended with the principle of sufficient reason in Theodicy; satirized as Dr. Pangloss by Voltaire
cards.work: Best of All Possible Worlds and the Theodicy

cards.front: Work: introduces the 'complete individual concept' — every substance's concept specifies everything that will ever happen to it, illustrated by Judas betraying Christ and Caesar crossing the Rubicon
cards.work: Discourse on Metaphysics and Truth Theory

cards.back: New Essays on Human Understanding (Gottfried Leibniz)

cards.front: Philosopher: corresponded with Samuel Clarke defending relational space against Newton's absolute space; used the identity of indiscernibles and 'static shift' arguments

Philosopher: the 'mill argument' imagines enlarging a thinking machine enough to walk inside it like a mill, finding only parts pushing each other — no perception; inspired Chalmers's philosophical zombie
Philosopher: Anne Conway's 'spirit particles' from Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy are believed to have inspired this thinker's concept of monads
Philosopher: introduced 'petites perceptions' (unconscious perceptions), illustrated by the sound of crashing waves being heard all together without distinguishing individual drops
Philosopher: distinguished necessary truths (grounded in non-contradiction) from contingent truths (grounded in the principle of sufficient reason)
